course_description:
Indigenous Language revitalisation champions build strong language communities through diverse language and cultural activities, growing language in use and making language more visible in public life. In this course, we examine ways that language planning, policy and status locally and globally open up or close down spaces for languages.

Students consolidate and apply knowledge and practical skills developed throughout the program and extend project design and management skills by creating a community-focussed language project.

This course students consolidate and apply knowledge and practical skills by developing a Community-focussed project, and position the project within the field of ILR policy and planning.

Before this happens, every effort will be made to enable students to voluntarily change into an alternative class that is suitable.
Please note: Teaching staff do not have access to the timetabling system to help with class allocation. Therefore, should you need help with your timetable and/or allocation of classes, please ensure you email slc.contact@uq.edu.au from your UQ student email account with the following details:

the Course Code

course_aims: This course aims to consolidate and apply knowledge and practical skills by developing a community-focused project, and position the project within the field of ILR policy and planning.

learning_outcomes.description: Explain connections between language policy, prestige, visibility and action.
Define a problem or need for a user group or audience in a Language Revitalisation context.
Scope resources, networks and opportunities, identify and manage risks, design strategies to meet project needs.

Review and evaluate strategies for a Language Revitalisation initiative using a range of tools and approaches, including peer and Community feedback, and reference to local and wider policy and goals.

assessment_summary.title: Review - Incorporating Feedback Into Your Proposal

assessment_details.title: Language Learning Journal
assessment_details.task_description: In this "Language Learning Journal" task, you will be asked to submit a reflection at three key stages of the course, 1) at the beginning of the course, 2) after the intensive, and 3) at the end of the course. Each entry should be approximately 200 words and guided by the questions on the journal topic. Statement on Generative AI & MT Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Please submit each of three journal entries through the journal assessment tab in Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Technology Review
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Review Technology for Language Revitalisation For this assignment, you will select and review a technology that supports language revitalisation, such as communication/distribution, learning, documentation, storage and archiving, transcription or analysis. Describe the requirements of the task and review one or more devices, programs or applications that suit the task. Include your own experience with the technology, learning and/or using it and any challenges or lessons you faced. Your review can take the form of a written review, with images/graphics as suits, word limit 700 words or a 5 minute in-class presentation or a video, which can include a demonstration. Time is allocated for presentations in the intensive. Videos can be uploaded to the Teams folder 'Assessment 1 - Videos'. Students who choose the option to present in class will complete their presentations on Wednesday 15 April during the intensive week. All other submissions are due on 22 April. A task guide and grading rubric is available in Blackboard. Statement on Generative AI & MT Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.

assessment_details.title: Project Proposal
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Language Revitalisation Project Proposal In this task you will develop a detailed proposal to undertake one or a set of activities, which contribute to language revitalisation in your community. Your proposal will include a brief and a detailed description, with goals, rationale, personnel, protocols, risks, delivery and monitoring plans. You will also identify and explain how your proposal aligns with policy and program funding criteria. To develop your proposal, you will share sections with other students (during the intensive) and members of your community, to gather feedback. This process and feedback will be used in the reflection task in Assessment 3. You will be provided a scaffolding guide to structure your proposal in Blackboard. You can choose to use the or develop an alternative structure, in discussion with teaching staff. Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course. Your project proposal should be between 2,400 to 3,000 words. Statement on Generative AI & MT Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Review - Incorporating Feedback Into Your Proposal
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: To develop the project proposal (Assessment 2), you will seek and receive feedback from other students in the course, and outside of the course, from members of your community. For this assessment task, you must document this process. You will document: your strategies for seeking feedback - who, how how your feedback was returned the content of the feedback you received your response feedback and if/how it was incorporated into your final project proposal. A task guide and grading rubric is available on Blackboard. You may submit a written review (maximum 700 words) or an oral reflection (4-5 minute video). Statement on Generative AI & MT Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. learning_activities.topic: Online Session Language Policy & Revitalisation in Action o Guest speaker – Clayton Cruse. Title tba o Discussion of Planning materials & activities for intensive

Online Session 1. Intro Technology for Language work What technologies do you/your community currently use in ILR? What technologies do you want to use? What advantages and challenges can you identify, from your experience and/or the reading Planning for intensive: Will you share a technology 2. Review: Linguistic Methods Comparing material from historic documents Discussion of paper by Mary Laughren (2017)

Intensive Day 1 Leadership and project planning – Introduction Language Policy and Planning – overview and approaches Language Policy and Planning as a field in applied linguistics; critical turn for minoritized languages –from theory to practice - Community Based Language Planning Workshopping project planning Topic 1 - Protocols for meetings and collaboration Topic 2 - Creating a statement of values and vision Technology in project planning 1 with Ben Foley From recording to publishing Common technologies at different project stages and purposes Assessment Planning – you project proposal

Intensive Day 2 Check in, review project planning sessions, project proposals Linguists on speed dial 1 Sounds and spelling with Katie Jepson Technology in project planning 2 with Ben Foley Making sound recordings and using recording equipment Technology in project planning 3 with Ben Foley Working with recordings – using [program] to edit, name and save Leadership and project planning Developing your project outline – 50 word pitch, giving and receiving feedback

Intensive Day 3 Leadership and project planning Workshopping project planning Topic 1 - Protocols for meetings and collaboration Topic 2 - Creating a statement of values and vision Linguists on Speed Dial 2 Ilana Mushin – Relationships between Languages and their importance for doing Language reclamation and revitalization Leadership, Language and project planning Monitoring and evaluating projects Developing your project - planning for community feedback Technology for Language projects Sharing technologies that you use Review Assignments

Intensive Day 4 Linguists on speed dial 3 with Mary Laughren - Making sense of historic written or audio materials/collections Leadership and project planning Planning your project - Addressing policy and program criteria Funding opportunities and grants Grant writing, grants management Global Perspectives on language revitalisation Sites world-wide of language reclamation Community-Centred action UN Decade of Indigenous Languages Opportunities for global networking

Intensive Day 5 Repurposing archival materials From list to dictionary From archive to gallery Partnerships with universities and institutions Phone a Friend Sharing our challenges and solutions Reviewing your network Identifying needs and solutions going forward Leadership and project planning Final push and share and give feedback on Project plans
Post-Workshop Online session 1 Review assessments Future planning Discuss selected readings/viewings
Post-Worshop Online session 2 Review assessments Upcoming events Reading for interest and sharing
Post-Worshop Online session 3 Review assessments Reading for interest and sharing
